---
title: "How RAG Works, Visually"
subtitle: "The mental model behind chunks, retrieval, and grounded answers"
author: "Pranjal Rawat"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: black
    transition: fade
    background-transition: fade
    incremental: false
    slide-number: c/t
    controls: true
    progress: true
    width: 1200
    height: 720
    margin: 0.055
    center: true
    navigation-mode: linear
---

```{=html}
<style>
:root {
  --bg: #16171a; --fg: #e6e6e9; --muted: #8b8b92; --sub: #a3a3ab;
  --accent: #7cb0de; --feat: #6bbf8a; --amber: #d9a441; --rule: #2a2c31;
  --node: #1d2733; --edge: #5a6472; --ink: #0f1114;
}
.reveal { font-family: -apple-system, BlinkMacSystemFont, "Segoe UI", Roboto, Helvetica, Arial, sans-serif; }
.reveal, .reveal .slides section { background: var(--bg); color: var(--fg); }
.reveal h1, .reveal h2, .reveal h3 { color: var(--fg); font-weight: 700; letter-spacing: -0.5px; text-transform: none; }
.reveal h2 { font-size: 1.55em; border-bottom: 2px solid var(--rule); padding-bottom: 0.25em; margin-bottom: 0.5em; }
.reveal a { color: var(--accent); }
.reveal .slide-number { background: none; color: var(--muted); }
.reveal .footer, .reveal .slide-number { font-size: 12px; }
.reveal strong { color: var(--accent); }
.reveal em { color: var(--sub); font-style: italic; }
.reveal .subtle { color: var(--sub); font-size: 0.8em; }
.reveal .lead { font-size: 1.15em; line-height: 1.5; }
.reveal code { color: var(--feat); background: rgba(124,176,222,0.08); padding: 1px 6px; border-radius: 4px; }
.reveal section img { background: none; border: none; box-shadow: none; }
/* SVG shared visual language */
.ragfig { width: 100%; height: auto; max-height: 62vh; display: block; margin: 0 auto; }
.node { fill: var(--node); stroke: var(--accent); stroke-width: 2.5; }
.node.feat { stroke: var(--feat); }
.node.merge { fill: #3a2f14; stroke: var(--amber); }
.node.ghost { fill: #191c20; stroke: var(--edge); }
.hash { fill: #cfe3f3; font: 600 16px ui-monospace, "SF Mono", Menlo, monospace; text-anchor: middle; }
.hash.dim { fill: var(--muted); }
.msg { fill: var(--sub); font: 15px -apple-system, sans-serif; text-anchor: middle; }
.cap { fill: var(--fg); font: 16px -apple-system, sans-serif; text-anchor: middle; }
.cap.big { font-size: 21px; font-weight: 600; }
.zonelabel { fill: var(--muted); font: 600 14px -apple-system, sans-serif; letter-spacing: 1.5px; text-anchor: middle; }
.edge { stroke: var(--edge); stroke-width: 2.5; fill: none; marker-end: url(#arrow); }
.flow { stroke: var(--accent); stroke-width: 2.5; fill: none; marker-end: url(#arrowA); stroke-dasharray: 6 5; }
.tag { rx: 5; }
.tag.main { fill: var(--accent); }
.tag.feat { fill: var(--feat); }
.tag.head { fill: var(--amber); }
.tagtext { fill: var(--ink); font: 700 14px ui-monospace, monospace; text-anchor: middle; }
.leader { stroke-width: 2; fill: none; }
.leader.main { stroke: var(--accent); }
.leader.feat { stroke: var(--feat); }
.leader.head { stroke: var(--amber); }
.zone { fill: none; stroke: var(--rule); stroke-width: 1.5; rx: 12; }
.card { fill: var(--node); stroke: var(--rule); stroke-width: 1.5; rx: 8; }
.card.hot { stroke: var(--amber); }
.cardtext { fill: var(--fg); font: 15px ui-monospace, monospace; }
.note { fill: var(--sub); font: 15px -apple-system, sans-serif; }
.reveal .fragment.grow-in { opacity: 0; transform: scale(0.9); transition: all 0.3s ease; }
.reveal .fragment.grow-in.visible { opacity: 1; transform: scale(1); }
/* draw-on edges: an edge draws itself in when revealed.
   pathLength="1" makes stroke-dasharray:1 span the whole path at any length. */
.edge.draw, .flow.draw { stroke-dasharray: 1; stroke-dashoffset: 1; }
.reveal .fragment.visible .edge.draw,
.reveal .fragment.visible .flow.draw,
.reveal .edge.draw.fragment.visible,
.reveal .flow.draw.fragment.visible { animation: gv-draw 0.55s ease both; }
@keyframes gv-draw { from { stroke-dashoffset: 1; } to { stroke-dashoffset: 0; } }
/* a node pops in about its own centre */
.reveal .fragment.pop { opacity: 0; transform-box: fill-box; transform-origin: center; }
.reveal .fragment.pop.visible { animation: gv-pop 0.45s cubic-bezier(0.2,0.8,0.2,1) both; }
@keyframes gv-pop { 0% { opacity: 0; transform: scale(0.5); } 60% { opacity: 1; transform: scale(1.06); } 100% { opacity: 1; transform: scale(1); } }
</style>

<svg width="0" height="0" style="position:absolute" aria-hidden="true">
  <defs>
    <marker id="arrow" viewBox="0 0 10 10" refX="8.5" refY="5" markerWidth="7" markerHeight="7" orient="auto">
      <path d="M0,0 L10,5 L0,10 z" fill="#5a6472"/>
    </marker>
    <marker id="arrowA" viewBox="0 0 10 10" refX="8.5" refY="5" markerWidth="7" markerHeight="7" orient="auto">
      <path d="M0,0 L10,5 L0,10 z" fill="#7cb0de"/>
    </marker>
  </defs>
</svg>
```

# The problem, and the fix {.center}

::: lead
Ask a plain language model a question about documents it has never seen, and it
will still answer. Confidently, and often wrong.
:::

Retrieval-augmented generation is the fix: look the answer up before writing it.

::: notes
This deck shows the mental model, not a library's API. By the end you should be able
to picture what happens, step by step, when a RAG system and an agent built on top of
it answer a question over a real corpus. The running example throughout is a fictional
company, Aurora Cloud, with 48 real documents: HR policy, product docs, engineering
runbooks, and some deliberately unrelated pages. Every figure in this deck is a real
run against that real corpus -- nothing is a mockup.
:::

---

## Ask it something it has never seen

A plain model has no way to check itself against real documents. It fills the gap with whatever is most probable, not what is true.

```{=html}
<svg class="ragfig" viewBox="0 0 1000 400">
  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="1">
    <rect class="card" x="40" y="165" width="270" height="70"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="60" y="196">"How many days of</text>
    <text class="cardtext" x="60" y="218">PTO do I get?"</text>
  </g>
  <path class="edge draw fragment" data-fragment-index="2" pathLength="1" d="M 310 200 L 385 200"/>
  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="2">
    <rect class="node" x="390" y="160" width="230" height="80" rx="10"/>
    <text class="cap" x="505" y="196">language model,</text>
    <text class="cap" x="505" y="220">alone</text>
  </g>
  <path class="flow draw fragment" data-fragment-index="3" pathLength="1" d="M 620 175 L 690 90"/>
  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="3">
    <rect class="card" x="700" y="60" width="240" height="60" style="stroke:var(--feat)"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="720" y="96" style="fill:var(--feat)">"15 days"</text>
  </g>
  <path class="flow draw fragment" data-fragment-index="4" pathLength="1" d="M 620 200 L 690 200"/>
  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="4">
    <rect class="card" x="700" y="170" width="240" height="60" style="stroke:var(--amber)"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="720" y="206" style="fill:var(--amber)">"20 days"</text>
  </g>
  <path class="flow draw fragment" data-fragment-index="5" pathLength="1" d="M 620 225 L 690 310"/>
  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="5">
    <rect class="card" x="700" y="280" width="240" height="60" style="stroke:var(--muted)"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="720" y="316" style="fill:var(--muted)">"not sure"</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment" data-fragment-index="6">
    <text class="cap big" x="500" y="30">Three answers, one question</text>
  </g>
</svg>
```

Same question, three different answers, and no way to tell which one is real.

::: notes
This is the hallucination problem in its plainest form: no grounding means no way to
adjudicate between guesses. Note the number changes each time -- that's a deliberate
callback to the near-duplicate PTO docs (hr-01 at 15 days, hr-02 at 20) that show up
later in the corpus.
:::

---

## Meet Aurora Cloud

Aurora Cloud is the fictional company behind every example in this deck: 48 documents across four topics.

```{=html}
<svg class="ragfig" viewBox="0 0 1000 380">
  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="1">
    <rect class="zone" x="40" y="60" width="210" height="220"/>
    <text class="zonelabel" x="145" y="90">HR</text>
    <text class="cap" x="145" y="170">PTO, expenses,</text>
    <text class="cap" x="145" y="192">leave policy</text>
    <text class="note" x="145" y="250" text-anchor="middle">12 docs</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="2">
    <rect class="zone" x="270" y="60" width="210" height="220"/>
    <text class="zonelabel" x="375" y="90">PRODUCT</text>
    <text class="cap" x="375" y="170">pricing, specs,</text>
    <text class="cap" x="375" y="192">release notes</text>
    <text class="note" x="375" y="250" text-anchor="middle">12 docs</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="3">
    <rect class="zone" x="500" y="60" width="210" height="220"/>
    <text class="zonelabel" x="605" y="90">ENGINEERING</text>
    <text class="cap" x="605" y="170">runbooks,</text>
    <text class="cap" x="605" y="192">architecture</text>
    <text class="note" x="605" y="250" text-anchor="middle">12 docs</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="4">
    <rect class="zone" x="730" y="60" width="210" height="220"/>
    <text class="zonelabel" x="835" y="90">OFF-TOPIC</text>
    <text class="cap" x="835" y="170">unrelated</text>
    <text class="cap" x="835" y="192">pages</text>
    <text class="note" x="835" y="250" text-anchor="middle">12 docs</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment" data-fragment-index="5">
    <text class="cap big" x="500" y="340">48 documents -- exactly what the model above never saw</text>
  </g>
</svg>
```

::: notes
This is the corpus every later slide in the deck runs against, for real: chunking,
embedding, retrieval, reranking, evaluation. Nothing here is a mockup -- it's the
same 48-document object every topic's run.py imports from corpus.py.
:::

---

## What retrieval changes

Retrieval-augmented generation adds one step in front of writing: look before you answer.

```{=html}
<svg class="ragfig" viewBox="0 0 1000 340">
  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="1">
    <rect class="card" x="20" y="140" width="180" height="70"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="40" y="180">question</text>
  </g>
  <path class="flow draw fragment" data-fragment-index="2" pathLength="1" d="M 200 175 L 275 175"/>
  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="2">
    <rect class="node" x="280" y="140" width="200" height="70" rx="10"/>
    <text class="cap" x="380" y="180">search the corpus</text>
  </g>
  <path class="flow draw fragment" data-fragment-index="3" pathLength="1" d="M 480 175 L 555 175"/>
  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="3">
    <rect class="card hot" x="560" y="140" width="190" height="70"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="580" y="172">retrieved</text>
    <text class="cardtext" x="580" y="194">chunk [1]</text>
  </g>
  <path class="flow draw fragment" data-fragment-index="4" pathLength="1" d="M 750 175 L 825 175"/>
  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="4">
    <rect class="node" x="830" y="115" width="140" height="120" rx="10" style="stroke:var(--feat)"/>
    <text class="cap" x="900" y="170" style="fill:var(--feat)">grounded</text>
    <text class="cap" x="900" y="192" style="fill:var(--feat)">answer</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment" data-fragment-index="5">
    <text class="cap big" x="500" y="60">Look the answer up, then write it</text>
  </g>
</svg>
```

The answer is no longer memory. It is a copy of what the corpus actually says.

::: notes
Search, cite, write -- three steps, each of which later topics in the deck open up
in detail (retrieval, reranking, prompt assembly). For now this is just the shape
of the fix.
:::

---

## The rule: cite or refuse

Grounding is a contract, not a suggestion. A covered question gets a citation. An uncovered one gets a decline, never a guess.

```{=html}
<svg class="ragfig" viewBox="0 0 1000 380">
  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="1">
    <rect class="card" x="380" y="20" width="240" height="60"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="400" y="56">any question</text>
  </g>
  <path class="flow draw fragment" data-fragment-index="2" pathLength="1" d="M 460 80 L 260 190"/>
  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="2">
    <rect class="card" x="90" y="195" width="320" height="70" style="stroke:var(--feat)"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="110" y="228" style="fill:var(--feat)">covered by a chunk</text>
    <text class="cardtext" x="110" y="250" style="fill:var(--feat)">-> answer + [1]</text>
  </g>
  <path class="flow draw fragment" data-fragment-index="3" pathLength="1" d="M 540 80 L 740 190"/>
  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="3">
    <rect class="card" x="590" y="195" width="320" height="70" style="stroke:var(--muted)"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="610" y="228" style="fill:var(--muted)">not covered</text>
    <text class="cardtext" x="610" y="250" style="fill:var(--muted)">-> decline, no guess</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment" data-fragment-index="4">
    <text class="cap big" x="500" y="330">Cite or refuse -- never a bare guess</text>
  </g>
</svg>
```

A refusal is a correct answer too, when the corpus genuinely doesn't cover it.

::: notes
This is the cite-or-refuse gate the rest of the deck builds toward: a similarity
threshold decides which branch a question takes. Topic 10 (generation) is where
this becomes a concrete number.
:::

---

<!--FB:the-problem|The Problem, and the Fix-->

---

# Two clocks, one system {.center}

::: lead
Building the index and answering a question are two different jobs, on two different clocks.
:::

One runs once, ahead of time. The other runs every time someone types a question.

::: notes
This is the map for the whole deck. Everything that follows -- chunking, embeddings, hybrid search, reranking, prompts, refusals -- is a closeup of one box in this picture. Keep this shape in mind and the details never get confusing about when they happen.
:::

---

## The corpus becomes a frozen index

The raw documents get chunked and turned into vectors a single time. After that, the index just sits there, ready to be searched.

```{=html}
<svg viewBox="0 0 1000 380" class="ragfig">
  <rect class="zone" x="30" y="60" width="940" height="260"/>
  <text class="zonelabel" x="500" y="92" text-anchor="middle">OFFLINE &middot; RUNS ONCE</text>

  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="1">
    <rect class="card" x="55" y="150" width="230" height="90"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="77" y="201" style="font-size:20px">corpus</text>
  </g>
  <path class="flow draw fragment" data-fragment-index="2" pathLength="1" d="M 285 195 L 393 195"/>
  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="2">
    <text class="note" x="340" y="175" text-anchor="middle" style="font-size:13px">chunk</text>
    <rect class="card" x="395" y="150" width="230" height="90"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="417" y="201" style="font-size:20px">chunks</text>
  </g>
  <path class="flow draw fragment" data-fragment-index="3" pathLength="1" d="M 625 195 L 733 195"/>
  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="3">
    <text class="note" x="680" y="175" text-anchor="middle" style="font-size:13px">embed + fit</text>
    <rect class="card" x="735" y="150" width="230" height="90"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="757" y="201" style="font-size:20px">index</text>
  </g>
  <text class="note fragment" x="500" y="285" text-anchor="middle">A batch job. Nothing here is waiting on a question.</text>
</svg>
```

::: notes
Notice there is no question anywhere in this picture. The corpus doesn't know what anyone is going to ask. It just gets chunked and indexed, once, and then it waits.
:::

---

## Every question walks the same four steps

A question comes in, gets turned into a search, and the results get folded into a prompt before the model ever runs.

```{=html}
<svg viewBox="0 0 1000 380" class="ragfig">
  <rect class="zone" x="20" y="60" width="960" height="260"/>
  <text class="zonelabel" x="500" y="92" text-anchor="middle">ONLINE &middot; RUNS PER QUESTION</text>

  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="1">
    <rect class="card" x="45" y="150" width="190" height="90"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="65" y="201" style="font-size:19px">query</text>
  </g>
  <path class="flow draw fragment" data-fragment-index="2" pathLength="1" d="M 235 195 L 281 195"/>
  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="2">
    <text class="note" x="258" y="175" text-anchor="middle" style="font-size:12px">search index</text>
    <rect class="card" x="285" y="150" width="190" height="90"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="305" y="201" style="font-size:19px">retrieve</text>
  </g>
  <path class="flow draw fragment" data-fragment-index="3" pathLength="1" d="M 475 195 L 521 195"/>
  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="3">
    <text class="note" x="498" y="175" text-anchor="middle" style="font-size:12px">build prompt</text>
    <rect class="card" x="525" y="150" width="190" height="90"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="545" y="201" style="font-size:19px">augment</text>
  </g>
  <path class="flow draw fragment" data-fragment-index="4" pathLength="1" d="M 715 195 L 761 195"/>
  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="4">
    <text class="note" x="738" y="175" text-anchor="middle" style="font-size:12px">call model</text>
    <rect class="card" x="765" y="150" width="190" height="90"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="785" y="201" style="font-size:19px">generate</text>
  </g>
  <text class="note fragment" x="500" y="285" text-anchor="middle">Same four steps, every single time someone asks.</text>
</svg>
```

::: notes
Retrieve, augment, generate: this is the whole online loop. No matter how the retrieval or the prompt gets fancier later in the deck, it still runs inside these four boxes.
:::

---

## Same shape, very different rhythm

Put both loops side by side and the contrast is the whole point: one column runs once, the other runs again for every question that comes in.

```{=html}
<svg viewBox="0 0 1000 620" class="ragfig">
  <rect class="zone" x="40" y="50" width="400" height="540"/>
  <text class="zonelabel" x="240" y="85" text-anchor="middle">OFFLINE</text>

  <rect class="zone" x="560" y="50" width="380" height="540"/>
  <text class="zonelabel" x="750" y="85" text-anchor="middle">ONLINE</text>

  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="1">
    <rect class="card" x="90" y="140" width="300" height="70"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="112" y="181" style="font-size:18px">corpus</text>
  </g>
  <path class="flow draw fragment" data-fragment-index="2" pathLength="1" d="M 240 210 L 240 258"/>
  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="2">
    <text class="note" x="270" y="238" style="font-size:12px">chunk</text>
    <rect class="card" x="90" y="260" width="300" height="70"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="112" y="301" style="font-size:18px">chunks</text>
  </g>
  <path class="flow draw fragment" data-fragment-index="3" pathLength="1" d="M 240 330 L 240 378"/>
  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="3">
    <text class="note" x="270" y="358" style="font-size:12px">embed + fit</text>
    <rect class="card" x="90" y="380" width="300" height="70"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="112" y="421" style="font-size:18px">index</text>
  </g>
  <text class="note fragment" x="240" y="505" text-anchor="middle">a batch job, ahead of time</text>

  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="4">
    <rect class="card" x="610" y="110" width="280" height="60"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="632" y="146" style="font-size:17px">query</text>
  </g>
  <path class="flow draw fragment" data-fragment-index="5" pathLength="1" d="M 750 170 L 750 203"/>
  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="5">
    <text class="note" x="800" y="192" style="font-size:11px">search index</text>
    <rect class="card" x="610" y="205" width="280" height="60"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="632" y="241" style="font-size:17px">retrieve</text>
  </g>
  <path class="flow draw fragment" data-fragment-index="6" pathLength="1" d="M 750 265 L 750 298"/>
  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="6">
    <text class="note" x="800" y="287" style="font-size:11px">build prompt</text>
    <rect class="card" x="610" y="300" width="280" height="60"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="632" y="336" style="font-size:17px">augment</text>
  </g>
  <path class="flow draw fragment" data-fragment-index="7" pathLength="1" d="M 750 360 L 750 393"/>
  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="7">
    <text class="note" x="800" y="382" style="font-size:11px">call model</text>
    <rect class="card" x="610" y="395" width="280" height="60"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="632" y="431" style="font-size:17px">generate</text>
  </g>
  <text class="note fragment" x="750" y="505" text-anchor="middle">runs again for every new question</text>

  <path class="flow draw fragment" data-fragment-index="8" pathLength="1" d="M 890 425 C 970 425, 970 140, 890 140"/>
  <text class="note fragment" data-fragment-index="8" x="995" y="282" text-anchor="end" style="font-size:12px">next question</text>
</svg>
```

::: notes
The left column happens once and then it's done. The right column is the loop -- it fires again for every question, always starting cold from the same frozen index. That's why it's called the naive RAG loop: nothing carries over between questions except the index itself.
:::

---

## This is the whole map

Everything from here on zooms into one box from this picture: how retrieval actually scores a match, how the prompt gets assembled, how the model is kept from making things up.

::: notes
Worth restating before diving into details: the split between "build the index" and "answer a question" is the single most useful fact to hold onto in this whole deck. Get that right and every later concept has an obvious home.
:::

<!--FB:the-naive-rag-loop|The Naive RAG Loop-->

---

# One document holds many facts {.center}

::: lead
A document mixes several separate facts into one block of text. A question only needs one of them back, not the whole page.
:::

```{=html}
<svg class="ragfig" viewBox="0 0 1000 400">
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="card" x="220" y="60" width="560" height="300"/>
    <text class="cap big" x="500" y="100" text-anchor="middle" style="fill:var(--accent)">Paid Time Off Policy v1</text>
    <rect class="card" x="260" y="130" width="480" height="50"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="284" y="160">15 days accrued per year</text>
    <rect class="card" x="260" y="195" width="480" height="50"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="284" y="225">carries over up to 5 days</text>
    <rect class="card" x="260" y="260" width="480" height="50"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="284" y="290">request 2 weeks ahead</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment pop">
    <rect class="card hot" x="260" y="130" width="480" height="50"/>
    <path class="leader head" d="M 750 155 L 815 155"/>
    <text class="cap" x="890" y="150" text-anchor="middle" style="fill:var(--amber)">the one line</text>
    <text class="cap" x="890" y="173" text-anchor="middle" style="fill:var(--amber)">a query needs</text>
  </g>
  <text class="note" x="500" y="385" text-anchor="middle">Retrieval works chunk by chunk -- it can only hand back what it indexed as one unit.</text>
</svg>
```

::: notes
This is the motivating problem for chunking. Indexed whole, this document returns three unrelated facts for any query that touches it. Chunked, a query about accrual only pulls the accrual sentence. This exact document (hr-01, "Paid Time Off Policy v1") is the one the walkthrough later splits for real.
:::

---

## Equal, non-overlapping cuts can break an idea

A window of fixed size slides across the words. The simplest version just cuts, edge to edge, with nothing shared.

```{=html}
<svg class="ragfig" viewBox="0 0 1000 400">
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="card" x="40" y="210" width="58" height="40"/>
    <rect class="card" x="106" y="210" width="58" height="40"/>
    <rect class="card" x="172" y="210" width="58" height="40"/>
    <rect class="card" x="238" y="210" width="58" height="40"/>
    <rect class="card" x="304" y="210" width="58" height="40"/>
    <rect class="card" x="370" y="210" width="58" height="40"/>
    <rect class="card" x="436" y="210" width="58" height="40"/>
    <rect class="card" x="502" y="210" width="58" height="40"/>
    <rect class="card" x="568" y="210" width="58" height="40"/>
    <rect class="card" x="634" y="210" width="58" height="40"/>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect x="36" y="185" width="330" height="90" style="fill:var(--accent); fill-opacity:0.08; stroke:var(--accent); stroke-width:3; rx:14"/>
    <text class="cap" x="201" y="165" text-anchor="middle" style="fill:var(--accent)">chunk 1</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect x="366" y="185" width="330" height="90" style="fill:var(--feat); fill-opacity:0.08; stroke:var(--feat); stroke-width:3; rx:14"/>
    <text class="cap" x="531" y="165" text-anchor="middle" style="fill:var(--feat)">chunk 2</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment pop">
    <path class="leader head" d="M 366 178 L 366 288" style="stroke-dasharray:8 6"/>
    <text class="note" x="366" y="320" text-anchor="middle" style="fill:var(--amber)">a fact split exactly here survives whole in neither chunk</text>
  </g>
</svg>
```

::: notes
This is the naive version of chunking: slide the window by its full size, no sharing. It works until an idea happens to straddle the cut -- then half of it ends up in chunk 1, half in chunk 2, and neither chunk contains the whole thing.
:::

---

## Overlap keeps the idea whole

Shift the window by less than its full size, and consecutive chunks share a few words at the seam.

```{=html}
<svg class="ragfig" viewBox="0 0 1000 400">
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="card" x="40" y="210" width="58" height="40"/>
    <rect class="card" x="106" y="210" width="58" height="40"/>
    <rect class="card" x="172" y="210" width="58" height="40"/>
    <rect class="card" x="238" y="210" width="58" height="40"/>
    <rect class="card" x="304" y="210" width="58" height="40"/>
    <rect class="card" x="370" y="210" width="58" height="40"/>
    <rect class="card" x="436" y="210" width="58" height="40"/>
    <rect class="card" x="502" y="210" width="58" height="40"/>
    <rect class="card" x="568" y="210" width="58" height="40"/>
    <rect class="card" x="634" y="210" width="58" height="40"/>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect x="36" y="185" width="396" height="90" style="fill:var(--accent); fill-opacity:0.08; stroke:var(--accent); stroke-width:3; rx:14"/>
    <text class="cap" x="234" y="165" text-anchor="middle" style="fill:var(--accent)">chunk 1</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect x="300" y="185" width="396" height="90" style="fill:var(--feat); fill-opacity:0.08; stroke:var(--feat); stroke-width:3; rx:14"/>
    <text class="cap" x="498" y="305" text-anchor="middle" style="fill:var(--feat)">chunk 2</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment pop">
    <rect x="300" y="185" width="132" height="90" style="fill:var(--amber); fill-opacity:0.22; stroke:var(--amber); stroke-width:2; rx:8"/>
    <text class="note" x="366" y="234" text-anchor="middle" style="fill:var(--amber); font-weight:600">overlap</text>
  </g>
  <text class="note" x="500" y="380" text-anchor="middle">The last words of chunk 1 are the first words of chunk 2 -- the idea at the seam survives in both.</text>
</svg>
```

::: notes
Same window, shifted by less than its width. The two chunks now share a band of words. Whatever idea lands in that band is complete in chunk 1 and complete again in chunk 2 -- overlap is just insurance against the cut in the previous slide. The walkthrough proves this against real words: literally `chunk_i.text.split()[-5:] == chunk_{i+1}.text.split()[:5]`.
:::

---

## Size is a tradeoff

A bigger window swallows unrelated ideas into one chunk. A smaller window slices a single idea into fragments that lose their surrounding context.

```{=html}
<svg class="ragfig" viewBox="0 0 1000 400">
  <g class="fragment pop">
    <rect class="card hot" x="60" y="90" width="400" height="240"/>
    <text class="cap" x="260" y="70" text-anchor="middle" style="fill:var(--amber)">size too big</text>
    <rect class="tag main" x="100" y="140" width="140" height="36"/>
    <text class="tagtext" x="170" y="163" text-anchor="middle">pricing</text>
    <rect class="tag feat" x="260" y="140" width="150" height="36"/>
    <text class="tagtext" x="335" y="163" text-anchor="middle">retention</text>
    <rect class="tag head" x="100" y="210" width="170" height="36"/>
    <text class="tagtext" x="185" y="233" text-anchor="middle">encryption</text>
    <text class="note" x="260" y="300" text-anchor="middle">three unrelated facts, one chunk</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment pop">
    <rect class="card hot" x="540" y="90" width="400" height="240"/>
    <text class="cap" x="740" y="70" text-anchor="middle" style="fill:var(--amber)">size too small</text>
    <rect class="card" x="560" y="150" width="100" height="40"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="568" y="175" style="font-size:13px">...costs 6</text>
    <rect class="card" x="680" y="150" width="100" height="40"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="688" y="175" style="font-size:13px">dollars a</text>
    <rect class="card" x="800" y="150" width="100" height="40"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="808" y="175" style="font-size:13px">month...</text>
    <path class="edge" d="M 660 170 L 680 170" style="stroke-dasharray:4 4"/>
    <path class="edge" d="M 780 170 L 800 170" style="stroke-dasharray:4 4"/>
    <text class="note" x="740" y="300" text-anchor="middle">one idea, chopped into pieces with no context</text>
  </g>
  <text class="note" x="500" y="370" text-anchor="middle">There is no universally right size -- it depends on how self-contained your documents' ideas are.</text>
</svg>
```

::: notes
Both failure modes are real. Too big, and a single chunk answers for three different questions, diluting what a retriever matches against. Too small, and a chunk stops being a coherent unit at all -- just a shard of a sentence. Aurora Cloud's pipeline uses size=40 with overlap=10 as its working default; the walkthrough shows what a much smaller window does instead.
:::

---

## Most of Aurora Cloud's documents need no splitting at all

Under the window this pipeline actually uses, nearly every one of the 48 documents collapses to a single chunk. Shrink the window, and one of them finally splits for real.

```{=html}
<svg class="ragfig" viewBox="0 0 1000 400">
  <g class="fragment">
    <text class="zonelabel" x="500" y="110" text-anchor="middle">48 SHORT DOCUMENTS</text>
    <rect class="card" x="300" y="150" width="40" height="90"/>
    <rect class="card" x="350" y="150" width="40" height="90"/>
    <rect class="card" x="400" y="150" width="40" height="90"/>
    <rect class="card" x="450" y="150" width="40" height="90"/>
    <rect class="card" x="500" y="150" width="40" height="90"/>
    <rect class="card hot" x="550" y="150" width="40" height="90"/>
    <rect class="card" x="600" y="150" width="40" height="90"/>
    <rect class="card" x="650" y="150" width="40" height="90"/>
    <rect class="card" x="700" y="150" width="40" height="90"/>
    <rect class="card" x="750" y="150" width="40" height="90"/>
    <rect class="card" x="800" y="150" width="40" height="90"/>
    <rect class="card" x="850" y="150" width="40" height="90"/>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment pop">
    <path class="leader head" d="M 570 244 L 570 280"/>
    <text class="cap" x="570" y="305" text-anchor="middle" style="fill:var(--amber)">this one splits</text>
  </g>
  <text class="note" x="500" y="370" text-anchor="middle">The walkthrough shrinks the window until a real document splits, then proves the overlap is real too.</text>
</svg>
```

::: notes
Every square here is one of Aurora Cloud's real documents. Under the pipeline's actual default (size=40, overlap=10), all 48 stay whole -- confirmed empirically, not assumed. The flipbook that follows shrinks the window to size=15, finds a document that really does split into multiple chunks, and checks the overlapping words against each other directly.
:::

<!--FB:chunking-the-corpus|Chunking the Corpus-->

---

# Embeddings are geometry {.center}

::: lead
A chunk of text turns into a list of numbers. That list is a location - somewhere you could point to on a map.
:::

Once meaning has a location, comparing meanings becomes measuring distance.

::: notes
This is the idea that makes every later concept (retrieval, hybrid search, reranking) possible. Before this, a "search" is a distance calculation.
:::

---

## A chunk becomes a point in space

```{=html}
<svg viewBox="0 0 1000 400" class="ragfig">
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="card" x="40" y="150" width="270" height="100"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="60" y="185">"requests for time off</text>
    <text class="cardtext" x="60" y="210">must be submitted two</text>
    <text class="cardtext" x="60" y="235">weeks in advance..."</text>
    <text class="cap" x="175" y="128">a chunk of text</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <path class="flow" d="M 316 200 L 380 200"/>
    <text class="note" x="348" y="182" text-anchor="middle" style="font-size:13px">vectorize</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="card" x="385" y="165" width="270" height="70"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="405" y="192">[0.02, 0.31, 0.00,</text>
    <text class="cardtext" x="405" y="217">0.18, 0.00, ...]</text>
    <text class="cap" x="520" y="128">a TF-IDF vector</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <path class="flow" d="M 661 200 L 725 200"/>
    <text class="note" x="693" y="182" text-anchor="middle" style="font-size:13px">project</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment pop">
    <circle class="node" cx="830" cy="200" r="34"/>
    <text class="cap big" x="830" y="128" style="fill:var(--accent)">a point in space</text>
  </g>
</svg>
```

::: notes
Any vectorizer works here: TF-IDF weighted word counts, or a neural embedding model. The pipeline in this deck uses TF-IDF (real, computed, not staged) because it is transparent - every coordinate traces back to actual words in the chunk.
:::

---

## Hundreds of numbers, two you can draw

```{=html}
<svg viewBox="0 0 1000 400" class="ragfig">
  <g class="fragment">
    <text class="cap" x="500" y="80" style="fill:var(--muted)">a real vector has hundreds of numbers</text>
    <rect class="card" x="120" y="120" width="34" height="34"/>
    <rect class="card" x="164" y="120" width="34" height="34"/>
    <rect class="card" x="208" y="120" width="34" height="34"/>
    <rect class="card" x="252" y="120" width="34" height="34"/>
    <text class="note" x="310" y="142">...</text>
    <rect class="card" x="360" y="120" width="34" height="34"/>
    <rect class="card" x="404" y="120" width="34" height="34"/>
    <rect class="card" x="448" y="120" width="34" height="34"/>
    <rect class="card" x="492" y="120" width="34" height="34"/>
    <text class="note" x="700" y="142">...</text>
    <rect class="card" x="750" y="120" width="34" height="34"/>
    <rect class="card" x="794" y="120" width="34" height="34"/>
    <rect class="card" x="838" y="120" width="34" height="34"/>
    <text class="note" x="500" y="192" text-anchor="middle">one number per dimension of meaning</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <path class="flow" d="M 500 210 L 500 250"/>
    <text class="note" x="500" y="238" text-anchor="middle" style="font-size:13px">keep the 2 that separate topics best</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment pop">
    <path d="M 350 355 L 350 270" style="stroke:var(--edge);stroke-width:1.5;fill:none"/>
    <path d="M 350 355 L 650 355" style="stroke:var(--edge);stroke-width:1.5;fill:none"/>
    <circle class="node" cx="500" cy="310" r="18"/>
    <text class="note" x="500" y="390" text-anchor="middle">a flat picture you can actually look at</text>
  </g>
</svg>
```

::: notes
This is the honest caveat: the pictures in this deck are a 2D projection (TruncatedSVD on the real TF-IDF matrix), not the literal embedding. A projection can't preserve every distance perfectly, but it preserves enough of the structure that near-in-2D reliably means near-in-reality here.
:::

---

## Related chunks land close together

```{=html}
<svg viewBox="0 0 1000 400" class="ragfig">
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="zone" x="185" y="175" width="220" height="150" style="stroke-dasharray:5 4"/>
    <circle class="node" cx="260" cy="220" r="26"/>
    <circle class="node" cx="330" cy="260" r="26"/>
    <circle class="node" cx="230" cy="290" r="26"/>
    <text class="note" x="270" y="180" text-anchor="middle">"PTO"</text>
    <text class="note" x="370" y="245" text-anchor="middle">"parental leave"</text>
    <text class="note" x="150" y="305" text-anchor="middle">"sick days"</text>
    <text class="cap" x="295" y="360" style="fill:var(--accent)">close together = related</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment pop">
    <circle class="node ghost" cx="800" cy="150" r="26"/>
    <text class="note" x="800" y="110" text-anchor="middle">"sharpen a kitchen knife"</text>
    <text class="cap" x="800" y="200" style="fill:var(--muted)">far away = unrelated</text>
  </g>
</svg>
```

::: notes
Three HR questions huddle together because they share vocabulary and topic. A kitchen-knife question shares almost none of that vocabulary, so it lands somewhere else entirely. This is the whole trick, drawn as plainly as possible.
:::

---

## Search by meaning is search by distance

```{=html}
<svg viewBox="0 0 1000 400" class="ragfig">
  <rect class="zone" x="185" y="175" width="220" height="150" style="stroke-dasharray:5 4"/>
  <circle class="node" cx="260" cy="220" r="24"/>
  <circle class="node" cx="330" cy="260" r="24"/>
  <circle class="node" cx="230" cy="290" r="24"/>
  <circle class="node ghost" cx="800" cy="150" r="24"/>
  <g class="fragment pop">
    <circle class="node merge" cx="300" cy="330" r="22"/>
    <text class="cap" x="300" y="378" style="fill:var(--amber)">query: "how many days off do I get"</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <path class="edge draw" pathLength="1" d="M 300 308 L 260 244" style="stroke:var(--amber);marker-end:none"/>
    <text class="note" x="140" y="270" style="fill:var(--amber);font-size:13px">nearest -> this is the answer</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <path d="M 320 315 L 780 172" style="stroke:var(--muted);stroke-width:1.5;stroke-dasharray:4 4;fill:none"/>
    <text class="note" x="640" y="230" style="fill:var(--muted);font-size:13px">far -> never considered</text>
  </g>
</svg>
```

The query gets vectorized exactly like every chunk, then dropped onto the same map. Whichever dot it lands nearest is the answer.

::: notes
This reframe is the payoff of the whole concept: "search by meaning" was always going to need a precise mechanical definition, and the definition turns out to be nearest-neighbor distance on this map. Retrieval (the next concept) is exactly this, run at real scale with a real distance metric (cosine similarity).
:::

---

## The next screens are the real thing {.center}

Not a mockup: the real 48-document Aurora Cloud corpus, chunked and turned into real TF-IDF vectors, projected to 2 axes. Each dot is a real chunk, colored by its document's tag. The star is a real query, placed by the same vectorizer, at its real projected position.

<!--FB:embeddings-as-geometry|Embeddings Are Geometry-->

---

# Building the Index {.center}

::: lead
Every chunk now has a vector. Those vectors don't sit around loose -- they
get packed together into one structure: the index. Building it happens
once. Answering a question against it never does.
:::

::: notes
This is the hinge between "embeddings as geometry" and "retrieval": once
each of the 48 chunks is a point in vector space, those points get stored
together as a single fitted object -- the TF-IDF matrix plus the vectorizer
that produced it. Fitting that object is a one-time cost. Every question
that follows, no matter how different, is just a lookup against the same
object.
:::

---

## Vectors get stored together

48 separate vectors. One structure holds all of them.

```{=html}
<svg class="ragfig" viewBox="0 0 1000 400">
  <g class="fragment pop">
    <circle class="node" cx="150" cy="120" r="16"/>
    <circle class="node" cx="230" cy="180" r="16"/>
    <circle class="node" cx="130" cy="240" r="16"/>
    <circle class="node" cx="270" cy="90"  r="16"/>
    <circle class="node" cx="190" cy="300" r="16"/>
    <circle class="node" cx="310" cy="230" r="16"/>
    <text class="cap" x="220" y="350">48 chunk vectors</text>
  </g>
  <path class="edge draw fragment" pathLength="1" d="M 162 126 L 620 195"/>
  <path class="edge draw fragment" pathLength="1" d="M 240 184 L 620 202"/>
  <path class="edge draw fragment" pathLength="1" d="M 140 243 L 620 210"/>
  <path class="edge draw fragment" pathLength="1" d="M 278 94  L 620 190"/>
  <path class="edge draw fragment" pathLength="1" d="M 200 302 L 620 216"/>
  <path class="edge draw fragment" pathLength="1" d="M 318 232 L 620 205"/>
  <g class="fragment pop">
    <rect class="card hot" x="620" y="150" width="280" height="100"/>
    <text class="cap big" x="760" y="207" text-anchor="middle">the index</text>
  </g>
  <text class="note" x="500" y="385" text-anchor="middle">Not 48 separate lookups later -- one shared structure, right now.</text>
</svg>
```

::: notes
Under the hood this is literally one `VectorIndex` object: a fitted
`TfidfVectorizer` plus the matrix of every chunk's vector, together. There
is no per-chunk index -- there is one index that happens to contain 48 rows.
:::

---

## Fit once, then query without limit

The fit is a single event. Every search after it is free to repeat.

```{=html}
<svg class="ragfig" viewBox="0 0 1000 400">
  <line x1="120" y1="230" x2="900" y2="230" style="stroke:var(--rule);stroke-width:2"/>
  <g class="fragment pop">
    <text class="zonelabel" x="220" y="90">BUILD -- ONCE</text>
    <circle class="node merge" cx="220" cy="230" r="28"/>
    <text class="cap" x="220" y="175">fit the index</text>
  </g>
  <path class="edge draw fragment" pathLength="1" d="M 248 230 L 900 230" style="stroke-dasharray:8 6"/>
  <text class="zonelabel" x="620" y="90">QUERY -- ANY NUMBER OF TIMES</text>
  <g class="fragment pop">
    <circle class="node" cx="400" cy="230" r="18"/>
    <text class="cap" x="400" y="290">search #1</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment pop">
    <circle class="node" cx="570" cy="230" r="18"/>
    <text class="cap" x="570" y="290">search #2</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment pop">
    <circle class="node" cx="740" cy="230" r="18"/>
    <text class="cap" x="740" y="290">search #3</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment pop">
    <text class="cap big" x="860" y="238" text-anchor="middle">...</text>
  </g>
  <text class="note" x="500" y="365" text-anchor="middle">The fit sits at the left edge, once. Everything to its right is the same lookup, repeated.</text>
</svg>
```

::: notes
This is the piece people miss: fitting (building the vocabulary, the
matrix, the whole structure) is asymptotically irrelevant once it has
happened, because it happens exactly once. Search cost is what recurs, and
search is cheap.
:::

---

## Many questions, one index

Three unrelated questions. One already-built index answers all of them.

```{=html}
<svg class="ragfig" viewBox="0 0 1000 400">
  <rect class="card hot" x="370" y="260" width="260" height="90"/>
  <text class="cap big" x="500" y="312" text-anchor="middle">the index</text>

  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="1">
    <rect class="card" x="40" y="40" width="300" height="80"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="60" y="70">"how many days of PTO</text>
    <text class="cardtext" x="60" y="94">do I get?"</text>
  </g>
  <path class="edge draw fragment" data-fragment-index="1" pathLength="1" d="M 200 120 L 415 260"/>

  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="2">
    <rect class="card" x="360" y="10" width="280" height="80"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="380" y="40">"what happens during a</text>
    <text class="cardtext" x="380" y="64">database outage?"</text>
  </g>
  <path class="edge draw fragment" data-fragment-index="2" pathLength="1" d="M 500 90 L 500 258"/>

  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="3">
    <rect class="card" x="660" y="40" width="300" height="80"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="680" y="70">"how do I sharpen a</text>
    <text class="cardtext" x="680" y="94">kitchen knife?"</text>
  </g>
  <path class="edge draw fragment" data-fragment-index="3" pathLength="1" d="M 800 120 L 585 260"/>

  <text class="note" x="500" y="385" text-anchor="middle">One PTO question, one outage question, one totally off-topic question -- same index, zero refits.</text>
</svg>
```

::: notes
The three questions are deliberately unrelated: an HR question, an
engineering question, and one the corpus barely covers. All three hit
`.search()` on the exact same fitted object built at the top of this
section.
:::

---

## The index never rebuilds

The wrong mental model refits per question. The right one fits once.

```{=html}
<svg class="ragfig" viewBox="0 0 1000 400">
  <text class="zonelabel" x="500" y="45" text-anchor="middle">NOT THIS</text>
  <g class="fragment pop">
    <rect class="node ghost" x="120" y="65" width="180" height="70"/>
    <text class="cap" x="210" y="105" text-anchor="middle">rebuild</text>
    <rect class="node ghost" x="410" y="65" width="180" height="70"/>
    <text class="cap" x="500" y="105" text-anchor="middle">rebuild</text>
    <rect class="node ghost" x="700" y="65" width="180" height="70"/>
    <text class="cap" x="790" y="105" text-anchor="middle">rebuild</text>
    <text class="note" x="500" y="160" text-anchor="middle">a fresh index per question -- thrown away every time</text>
  </g>

  <line x1="80" y1="195" x2="920" y2="195" style="stroke:var(--rule);stroke-width:1.5;stroke-dasharray:4 4"/>

  <text class="zonelabel" x="500" y="235" text-anchor="middle">THIS</text>
  <g class="fragment pop">
    <rect class="card hot" x="410" y="250" width="180" height="70"/>
    <text class="cap" x="500" y="290" text-anchor="middle">one index</text>
    <path class="flow" d="M 480 250 C 380 205, 260 295, 210 320"/>
    <path class="flow" d="M 500 250 L 500 320"/>
    <path class="flow" d="M 520 250 C 620 205, 740 295, 790 320"/>
    <text class="cap" x="210" y="345" text-anchor="middle">search</text>
    <text class="cap" x="500" y="345" text-anchor="middle">search</text>
    <text class="cap" x="790" y="345" text-anchor="middle">search</text>
  </g>
  <text class="note" x="500" y="385" text-anchor="middle">Built once, searched as many times as there are questions. Next: watch it happen for real.</text>
</svg>
```

::: notes
The failure mode to rule out: treating "build the index" as a step inside
the query loop. It isn't. It runs once, before the first question ever
arrives, and every subsequent question is a pure read against it.
:::

<!--FB:building-the-index|Building the Index-->

---

# Retrieval: The Nearest Points Win {.center}

::: lead
The index holds 48 points. Retrieval is the one operation that matters: drop the
query onto the same map, and read off whichever points sit closest.
:::

Nothing about retrieval is mysterious once the geometry is in place. It's a distance
calculation, run once per question.

::: notes
This is the payoff of "embeddings are geometry" and "building the index." Both of
those concepts existed to make this one moment possible: a question arrives, and
answering it becomes "which points are nearest," not "which document sounds right."
:::

---

## From a question to a ranked list

```{=html}
<svg class="ragfig" viewBox="0 0 1000 380">
  <g class="fragment pop">
    <rect class="card" x="60" y="150" width="260" height="70"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="80" y="182">"how many days of</text>
    <text class="cardtext" x="80" y="204">PTO do I get?"</text>
  </g>
  <path class="flow draw fragment" pathLength="1" d="M 320 185 L 390 185"/>
  <g class="fragment pop">
    <text class="note" x="355" y="165" text-anchor="middle" style="font-size:13px">vectorize</text>
    <rect class="node" x="395" y="150" width="180" height="70" rx="10"/>
    <text class="cap" x="485" y="192" text-anchor="middle">query vector</text>
  </g>
  <path class="flow draw fragment" pathLength="1" d="M 575 185 L 645 185"/>
  <g class="fragment pop">
    <text class="note" x="610" y="165" text-anchor="middle" style="font-size:13px">compare</text>
    <rect class="node" x="650" y="150" width="180" height="70" rx="10"/>
    <text class="cap" x="740" y="192" text-anchor="middle">48 chunk scores</text>
  </g>
  <path class="flow draw fragment" pathLength="1" d="M 830 185 L 900 185"/>
  <g class="fragment pop">
    <rect class="card hot" x="905" y="150" width="70" height="70"/>
    <text class="cap" x="940" y="192" text-anchor="middle">top-k</text>
  </g>
  <text class="note fragment" x="500" y="300" text-anchor="middle">Every chunk gets a score. Only the highest few ever get shown to anyone.</text>
</svg>
```

::: notes
The query is embedded through the exact same vectorizer that embedded every chunk --
that's what makes the comparison meaningful. Cosine similarity scores all 48 chunks
at once; sorting and slicing to the top-k is the only step left.
:::

---

## Nearest wins, and "nearest" is a number

```{=html}
<svg class="ragfig" viewBox="0 0 1000 360">
  <circle class="node merge" cx="500" cy="180" r="24"/>
  <text class="cap" x="500" y="140" text-anchor="middle" style="fill:var(--amber)">query</text>
  <g class="fragment">
    <circle class="node" cx="330" cy="140" r="18"/>
    <path class="edge draw" pathLength="1" d="M 480 190 L 348 148"/>
    <text class="note" x="300" y="110" text-anchor="middle" style="fill:var(--feat)">0.481 -- close</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <circle class="node" cx="740" cy="230" r="18"/>
    <path class="edge draw" pathLength="1" d="M 520 195 L 725 226"/>
    <text class="note" x="770" y="270" text-anchor="middle" style="fill:var(--muted)">0.068 -- far</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <circle class="node" cx="620" cy="90" r="18"/>
    <path class="edge draw" pathLength="1" d="M 510 160 L 610 100"/>
    <text class="note" x="660" y="70" text-anchor="middle" style="fill:var(--muted)">0.062 -- far</text>
  </g>
  <text class="cap big fragment" x="500" y="330" text-anchor="middle">Rank by score, take the top few, done.</text>
</svg>
```

::: notes
There's no threshold logic inside retrieval itself -- it always returns its top-k,
confidently, whether the best score is 0.48 or 0.02. Deciding what to do with a weak
top score is generation's job (cite or refuse), a few concepts from now.
:::

---

## The next screens are the real thing

Not a mockup: the real 48-document Aurora Cloud corpus, the real fitted index from
the last concept, one real query run through `.search()`. The ranked list, the
scores, and the map are all computed live.

::: notes
Watch the same query land in the embedding space right where the top-ranked hr
documents sit, and watch a second, uncovered question come back with uniformly low
scores -- retrieval still answers, it just isn't confident about any of it.
:::

<!--FB:retrieval|Retrieval: The Nearest Points Win-->

---

# One ranking isn't enough {.center}

::: lead
Dense search ranks by meaning. Sparse search ranks by exact words. Run the same
query through both and they don't agree -- fusing the two beats trusting either alone.
:::

---

## Dense vs. sparse: two different notions of "relevant"

```{=html}
<svg class="ragfig" viewBox="0 0 1000 400">
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="zone" x="40" y="55" width="430" height="300"/>
    <text class="zonelabel" x="255" y="90" text-anchor="middle" style="fill:var(--accent)">DENSE -- COSINE SIMILARITY</text>
    <text class="note" x="118" y="155">query</text>
    <circle class="node" cx="150" cy="205" r="34"/><text class="hash" x="150" y="211">Q</text>
    <circle class="node" cx="365" cy="150" r="30" style="stroke:var(--amber)"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="365" y="127" text-anchor="middle">Pricing</text>
    <circle class="node" cx="365" cy="270" r="30" style="stroke:var(--feat)"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="365" y="313" text-anchor="middle">Escalation</text>
    <path class="edge" d="M 182 192 L 336 158"/>
    <text class="note" x="255" y="160" style="font-size:13px">0.28</text>
    <path class="edge" d="M 182 216 L 336 262"/>
    <text class="note" x="255" y="255" style="font-size:13px">0.16</text>
    <text class="note" x="255" y="335" text-anchor="middle">scores every doc by overall topical closeness</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="zone" x="530" y="55" width="430" height="300"/>
    <text class="zonelabel" x="745" y="90" text-anchor="middle" style="fill:var(--feat)">SPARSE -- BM25</text>
    <rect class="card" x="565" y="120" width="105" height="42"/><text class="cardtext" x="617" y="146" text-anchor="middle">notify</text>
    <rect class="card" x="680" y="120" width="120" height="42" style="stroke:var(--feat)"/><text class="cardtext" x="740" y="146" text-anchor="middle" style="fill:var(--feat)">escalation</text>
    <rect class="card" x="810" y="120" width="105" height="42"/><text class="cardtext" x="862" y="146" text-anchor="middle">on-call</text>
    <path class="edge" d="M 740 162 L 740 228" style="stroke:var(--feat)"/>
    <rect class="card" x="650" y="235" width="180" height="55" style="stroke:var(--feat)"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="740" y="268" text-anchor="middle" style="fill:var(--feat)">Escalation Policy</text>
    <text class="note" x="745" y="335" text-anchor="middle">exact term match wins, regardless of topic overlap</text>
  </g>
</svg>
```

::: notes
Dense turns the query into a vector and measures how close it lands to every doc's
vector -- a graded, continuous notion of "about the same thing." Sparse never leaves
the words: it rewards a document for containing the query's rare terms, "escalation"
and "on-call" here, whether or not the rest of the document is topically similar.
:::

---

## Same query, two different rankings

```{=html}
<svg class="ragfig" viewBox="0 0 1000 400">
  <g class="fragment">
    <text class="cap big" x="140" y="50" text-anchor="middle" style="fill:var(--accent)">DENSE</text>
    <rect class="card" x="10" y="80" width="260" height="60" style="stroke:var(--amber)"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="30" y="116">#1</text><text class="cardtext" x="90" y="116" style="fill:var(--amber)">Pricing</text>
    <rect class="card" x="10" y="170" width="260" height="60"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="30" y="206">#2</text><text class="cardtext" x="90" y="206">Overview</text>
    <rect class="card" x="10" y="260" width="260" height="60" style="stroke:var(--feat)"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="30" y="296">#3</text><text class="cardtext" x="90" y="296" style="fill:var(--feat)">Escalation Policy</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <text class="cap big" x="860" y="50" text-anchor="middle" style="fill:var(--feat)">SPARSE</text>
    <rect class="card" x="730" y="80" width="260" height="60" style="stroke:var(--feat)"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="750" y="116">#1</text><text class="cardtext" x="810" y="116" style="fill:var(--feat)">Escalation Policy</text>
    <rect class="card" x="730" y="170" width="260" height="60" style="stroke:var(--amber)"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="750" y="206">#2</text><text class="cardtext" x="810" y="206" style="fill:var(--amber)">Pricing</text>
    <rect class="card" x="730" y="260" width="260" height="60"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="750" y="296">#3</text><text class="cardtext" x="810" y="296">Overview</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <path class="edge" d="M 270 110 C 450 110, 550 196, 730 196" style="stroke:var(--amber);stroke-dasharray:5 4"/>
    <path class="edge" d="M 270 200 C 450 200, 550 286, 730 286" style="stroke:var(--accent);stroke-dasharray:5 4"/>
    <path class="edge" d="M 270 290 C 450 290, 550 110, 730 110" style="stroke:var(--feat);stroke-dasharray:5 4"/>
    <text class="note" x="500" y="370" text-anchor="middle">Same corpus, same query -- Escalation Policy jumps from #3 to #1.</text>
  </g>
</svg>
```

::: notes
Nobody changed the corpus or the query between these two lists. The only thing that
changed is which notion of "relevant" is doing the ranking, and that alone reorders
the results. Neither list is wrong; they're answering slightly different questions.
:::

---

## Fusion: combine by rank, not raw score

```{=html}
<svg class="ragfig" viewBox="0 0 1000 400">
  <g class="fragment">
    <text class="cap big" x="140" y="50" text-anchor="middle" style="fill:var(--accent)">DENSE</text>
    <rect class="card" x="10" y="88" width="260" height="54" style="stroke:var(--amber)"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="30" y="115">#1</text><text class="cardtext" x="90" y="115" style="fill:var(--amber)">Pricing</text>
    <rect class="card" x="10" y="178" width="260" height="54"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="30" y="205">#2</text><text class="cardtext" x="90" y="205">Overview</text>
    <rect class="card" x="10" y="268" width="260" height="54" style="stroke:var(--feat)"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="30" y="295">#3</text><text class="cardtext" x="90" y="295" style="fill:var(--feat)">Escalation</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <text class="cap big" x="500" y="50" text-anchor="middle" style="fill:var(--feat)">SPARSE</text>
    <rect class="card" x="370" y="88" width="260" height="54" style="stroke:var(--feat)"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="390" y="115">#1</text><text class="cardtext" x="450" y="115" style="fill:var(--feat)">Escalation</text>
    <rect class="card" x="370" y="178" width="260" height="54" style="stroke:var(--amber)"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="390" y="205">#2</text><text class="cardtext" x="450" y="205" style="fill:var(--amber)">Pricing</text>
    <rect class="card" x="370" y="268" width="260" height="54"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="390" y="295">#3</text><text class="cardtext" x="450" y="295">Overview</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <path class="edge" d="M 270 115 C 550 115, 600 115, 730 115" style="stroke:var(--amber)"/>
    <path class="edge" d="M 630 205 C 690 205, 700 130, 730 118" style="stroke:var(--amber)"/>
    <path class="edge" d="M 270 295 C 550 295, 600 220, 730 208" style="stroke:var(--feat)"/>
    <path class="edge" d="M 630 115 C 690 115, 700 190, 730 205" style="stroke:var(--feat)"/>
    <path class="edge" d="M 270 205 C 550 205, 600 290, 730 298" style="stroke:var(--accent)"/>
    <path class="edge" d="M 630 295 C 690 295, 700 300, 730 298" style="stroke:var(--accent)"/>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <text class="cap big" x="860" y="50" text-anchor="middle">FUSED</text>
    <rect class="card" x="730" y="88" width="260" height="54" style="stroke:var(--amber)"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="750" y="115">#1</text><text class="cardtext" x="810" y="115" style="fill:var(--amber)">Pricing</text>
    <rect class="card" x="730" y="178" width="260" height="54" style="stroke:var(--feat)"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="750" y="205">#2</text><text class="cardtext" x="810" y="205" style="fill:var(--feat)">Escalation</text>
    <rect class="card" x="730" y="268" width="260" height="54" style="stroke:var(--accent)"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="750" y="295">#3</text><text class="cardtext" x="810" y="295">Overview</text>
    <text class="note" x="500" y="375" text-anchor="middle">score(chunk) = 1/(60 + rank_dense) + 1/(60 + rank_sparse) -- rank position only, never raw score</text>
  </g>
</svg>
```

::: notes
Reciprocal rank fusion never looks at the raw cosine similarity or the raw BM25
score, only at where each chunk landed in each list. That sidesteps a real problem:
the two scores live on incomparable scales, so averaging them directly would let
whichever scale happens to be bigger quietly dominate. A chunk that's decent in
both rankings can out-rank a chunk that's great in one and absent from the other.
:::

---

## Why fusion is more robust than either alone

```{=html}
<svg class="ragfig" viewBox="0 0 1000 400">
  <g class="fragment">
    <circle cx="380" cy="200" r="170" style="fill:var(--accent);fill-opacity:0.10;stroke:var(--accent);stroke-width:2.5"/>
    <text class="cap big" x="260" y="105" style="fill:var(--accent)">DENSE</text>
    <text class="note" x="150" y="335">paraphrases, synonyms, "what happens when..."</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <circle cx="620" cy="200" r="170" style="fill:var(--feat);fill-opacity:0.10;stroke:var(--feat);stroke-width:2.5"/>
    <text class="cap big" x="655" y="105" text-anchor="end" style="fill:var(--feat)">SPARSE</text>
    <text class="note" x="855" y="335" text-anchor="end">product names, IDs, exact jargon</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <text class="cap big" x="500" y="215" text-anchor="middle" style="fill:var(--amber);font-weight:700">FUSED</text>
    <text class="note" x="500" y="385" text-anchor="middle">doesn't have to guess which case the query is</text>
  </g>
</svg>
```

::: notes
Neither method is strictly better; each has a blind spot the other one covers.
Dense misses a rare product name it's never seen phrased that way; sparse misses
a paraphrase that shares no words with the document. Fusing them means the system
doesn't have to bet on which failure mode this particular query will hit.
:::

<!--FB:hybrid-search|Hybrid Search-->

---

# Reranking: A Second, More Careful Pass {.center}

::: lead
Retrieval scores every chunk against the query with one cheap, global signal, across
the whole corpus at once. That is what makes it fast, and it is also why the order it
returns is only approximately right.
:::

Reranking takes just the candidates retrieval already found and looks at each one
again, one at a time, with a narrower, more literal question: how much of the query's
actual wording is in this chunk.

::: notes
Set up the handoff cleanly before showing anything moving. Retrieval already ran and
already narrowed 48 documents down to 5 candidates -- reranking never sees the other
43. It is a second pass over a short list, not a second search.
:::

---

## The First Pass Is Approximate

A single global signal, spread across every word in the query, does not always put
the right document on top.

```{=html}
<svg class="ragfig" viewBox="0 0 1000 400">
  <text class="zonelabel" x="230" y="55">RETRIEVAL'S ORDER</text>
  <text class="zonelabel" x="770" y="55">A CLOSER LOOK</text>

  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="1">
    <rect class="card hot" x="70" y="80" width="320" height="64"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="95" y="107">#1 &middot; candidate A</text>
    <text class="cardtext" x="95" y="128" style="fill:var(--muted);font-size:12px">matches many query words a little</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="1">
    <rect class="card" x="70" y="164" width="320" height="64"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="95" y="191">#2 &middot; candidate B</text>
    <text class="cardtext" x="95" y="212" style="fill:var(--muted);font-size:12px">matches one query word exactly</text>
  </g>

  <path class="edge draw fragment" data-fragment-index="2" pathLength="1" d="M 410 112 C 500 112, 500 228, 610 228"/>
  <path class="edge draw fragment" data-fragment-index="2" pathLength="1" d="M 410 196 C 500 196, 500 112, 610 112"/>

  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="2">
    <rect class="card" x="610" y="80" width="320" height="64"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="635" y="107">#1 &middot; candidate B</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="2">
    <rect class="card hot" x="610" y="164" width="320" height="64"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="635" y="191">#2 &middot; candidate A</text>
  </g>

  <text class="note" x="500" y="360" text-anchor="middle">Same two candidates. Scored one at a time, the exact match wins.</text>
</svg>
```

::: notes
This slide is the abstract version of the mechanism, no real numbers yet. A doc that
scores well by scattering partial matches across many query words can still lose to a
doc with one precise match, once each is judged on its own instead of against the
whole corpus at once.
:::

---

## What Actually Happened: "on-call handoff day"

Tried the near-duplicate pairs first (PTO days, expense threshold, outage lag) --
none of them swap, because the one word that tells those two docs apart never appears
in the query. This query, tried next, produces a real swap at #1.

```{=html}
<svg class="ragfig" viewBox="0 0 1000 400">
  <text class="cap big" x="500" y="40" text-anchor="middle">query: "on-call handoff day"</text>
  <text class="zonelabel" x="230" y="80">BEFORE &middot; TF-IDF COSINE</text>
  <text class="zonelabel" x="770" y="80">AFTER &middot; KEYWORD OVERLAP</text>

  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="1">
    <rect class="card hot" x="60" y="100" width="340" height="66"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="85" y="128">#1 Onboarding Checklist</text>
    <text class="cardtext" x="85" y="150" style="fill:var(--amber);font-size:13px">score 0.208</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="1">
    <rect class="card" x="60" y="186" width="340" height="66"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="85" y="214">#2 On-call Rotation Schedule</text>
    <text class="cardtext" x="85" y="236" style="fill:var(--muted);font-size:13px">score 0.138</text>
  </g>

  <path class="edge draw fragment" data-fragment-index="2" pathLength="1" d="M 420 133 C 520 133, 520 253, 600 253"/>
  <path class="edge draw fragment" data-fragment-index="2" pathLength="1" d="M 420 219 C 520 219, 520 133, 600 133"/>

  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="2">
    <rect class="card hot" x="600" y="100" width="340" height="66"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="625" y="128">#1 On-call Rotation Schedule</text>
    <text class="cardtext" x="625" y="150" style="fill:var(--amber);font-size:13px">score 0.557</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="2">
    <rect class="card" x="600" y="186" width="340" height="66"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="625" y="214">#2 Onboarding Checklist</text>
    <text class="cardtext" x="625" y="236" style="fill:var(--muted);font-size:13px">score 0.371</text>
  </g>

  <text class="note" x="500" y="330" text-anchor="middle">"Onboarding Checklist" repeats the generic word "day" twice.</text>
  <text class="note" x="500" y="352" text-anchor="middle">Only "On-call Rotation Schedule" contains the exact word "handoff" -- reranking promotes it.</text>
</svg>
```

::: notes
This is the real run, not a mockup: `index.search("on-call handoff day", k=5)` then
`rerank(...)` on those same five results, in `build/topics/reranking/run.py`. TF-IDF's
top hit is genuinely the wrong document for this query. The reranker's plain
exact-word-count fixes it, and it is honest to say why: "day" is common and diluted
by idf, "handoff" is rare and, once scored on its own per chunk, decisive.
:::

---

## Reranking Reorders. It Does Not Fetch.

```{=html}
<svg class="ragfig" viewBox="0 0 1000 400">
  <rect class="zone" x="60" y="100" width="380" height="220"/>
  <text class="zonelabel" x="250" y="130">5 CANDIDATES</text>
  <circle class="node" cx="150" cy="220" r="26"/>
  <circle class="node" cx="220" cy="220" r="26"/>
  <circle class="node feat" cx="290" cy="220" r="26"/>
  <circle class="node" cx="360" cy="220" r="26"/>
  <circle class="node" cx="150" cy="270" r="0"/>

  <path class="flow" d="M 460 210 L 555 210"/>
  <text class="note" x="507" y="190" text-anchor="middle" style="font-size:13px">rerank()</text>

  <rect class="zone" x="560" y="100" width="380" height="220"/>
  <text class="zonelabel" x="750" y="130">SAME 5, REORDERED</text>
  <circle class="node feat" cx="650" cy="220" r="26"/>
  <circle class="node" cx="720" cy="220" r="26"/>
  <circle class="node" cx="790" cy="220" r="26"/>
  <circle class="node" cx="860" cy="220" r="26"/>

  <text class="note" x="500" y="360" text-anchor="middle">No new document ever enters. Reranking can only work with what retrieval already found.</text>
</svg>
```

::: notes
Ties directly to the assert in run.py: `set(ids_before) == set(ids_after)`. Worth
saying plainly -- if retrieval missed the right document entirely, no amount of
reranking gets it back. That is still retrieval's job, not reranking's.
:::

---

## What Reranking Can't Fix

The corpus has three near-duplicate pairs, same wording, one changed number. None of
them swap under this reranker.

```{=html}
<svg class="ragfig" viewBox="0 0 1000 400">
  <rect class="card" x="230" y="110" width="300" height="90"/>
  <text class="cardtext" x="255" y="145">Paid Time Off Policy v1</text>
  <text class="cardtext" x="255" y="170" style="fill:var(--muted);font-size:13px">"...accrue <tspan style="fill:var(--fg)">15</tspan> days..."</text>

  <rect class="card" x="470" y="110" width="300" height="90"/>
  <text class="cardtext" x="495" y="145">Paid Time Off Policy v2</text>
  <text class="cardtext" x="495" y="170" style="fill:var(--muted);font-size:13px">"...accrue <tspan style="fill:var(--fg)">20</tspan> days..."</text>

  <g class="fragment">
    <text class="cap" x="500" y="250" text-anchor="middle">query: "how many days of paid time off"</text>
    <text class="note" x="500" y="280" text-anchor="middle">Neither "15" nor "20" is in the query, so both chunks overlap it identically.</text>
    <text class="note" x="500" y="304" text-anchor="middle">The tie always breaks back to whichever retrieval already ranked first.</text>
  </g>
</svg>
```

That is an honest limit, not a bug: a reranker that only counts word overlap can't
tell two documents apart on the one word neither of them is being asked about.

::: notes
Confirmed empirically, not assumed: run the PTO query and the pair's relative order
is identical before and after reranking, down to the score (both land on the same
overlap count and the same tie-break). Whatever actually resolves this pair -- picking
one canonical version, deduplication upstream -- is a different concept's job, not
reranking's.
:::

<!--FB:reranking|Reranking-->

---

# The chunks aren't the input {.center}

::: lead
Retrieval hands back three objects. The model reads one string. Something has to build that string.
:::

```{=html}
<svg class="ragfig" viewBox="0 0 1000 380">
  <rect class="zone" x="40" y="70" width="920" height="230"/>
  <text class="zonelabel" x="500" y="100" text-anchor="middle">RETRIEVAL'S OUTPUT</text>
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="card" x="80" y="130" width="260" height="120" style="stroke:var(--accent)"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="100" y="168">Paid Time Off</text>
    <text class="cardtext" x="100" y="192">Policy v2</text>
    <text class="note" x="100" y="226">score 0.481</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="card" x="370" y="130" width="260" height="120" style="stroke:var(--feat)"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="390" y="168">Paid Time Off</text>
    <text class="cardtext" x="390" y="192">Policy v1</text>
    <text class="note" x="390" y="226">score 0.480</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="card" x="660" y="130" width="260" height="120" style="stroke:var(--amber)"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="680" y="168">Remote Work</text>
    <text class="cardtext" x="680" y="192">Guidelines</text>
    <text class="note" x="680" y="226">score 0.068</text>
  </g>
  <text class="cap" x="500" y="335" text-anchor="middle">a title, a score, a wall of text -- three separate objects</text>
</svg>
```

A chat model takes plain text in. Three `Chunk` objects are not plain text yet.

::: notes
Every retrieval call in this deck so far has ended at a ranked list of chunk objects. That list is still structured data: a doc title, a score, a body of text. Before generation can run, something has to flatten that into the one string a language model actually consumes.
:::

---

## assemble_prompt() turns them into one string

The retrieved chunks flow into a single card, and each one gains a number as it lands.

```{=html}
<svg class="ragfig" viewBox="0 0 1000 400">
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="card" x="60" y="30" width="240" height="60" style="stroke:var(--accent)"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="80" y="66">PTO Policy v2</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="card" x="380" y="30" width="240" height="60" style="stroke:var(--feat)"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="400" y="66">PTO Policy v1</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="card" x="700" y="30" width="240" height="60" style="stroke:var(--amber)"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="720" y="66">Remote Work Guidelines</text>
  </g>

  <path class="flow draw fragment" pathLength="1" d="M 180 92 L 260 195"/>
  <path class="flow draw fragment" pathLength="1" d="M 500 92 L 500 195"/>
  <path class="flow draw fragment" pathLength="1" d="M 820 92 L 740 195"/>

  <text class="cap big" x="500" y="178" text-anchor="middle" style="fill:var(--amber)">assemble_prompt()</text>
  <rect class="card hot" x="120" y="195" width="760" height="170"/>

  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="tag main" x="150" y="222" width="46" height="30"/>
    <text class="tagtext" x="173" y="243">[1]</text>
    <text class="cardtext" x="210" y="243">PTO Policy v2, excerpt</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="tag feat" x="150" y="264" width="46" height="30"/>
    <text class="tagtext" x="173" y="285">[2]</text>
    <text class="cardtext" x="210" y="285">PTO Policy v1, excerpt</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="tag head" x="150" y="306" width="46" height="30"/>
    <text class="tagtext" x="173" y="327">[3]</text>
    <text class="cardtext" x="210" y="327">Remote Work Guidelines, excerpt</text>
  </g>
</svg>
```

::: notes
This is the "A" in RAG. `assemble_prompt()` is a plain string builder: it walks the chunk list in order and writes each one out behind a bracketed number. Nothing clever happens here, and that is the point -- the model's grounding depends on this step being completely mechanical and inspectable.
:::

---

## Every excerpt is numbered, not named

```{=html}
<svg class="ragfig" viewBox="0 0 1000 320">
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="card" x="120" y="60" width="360" height="70" style="stroke:var(--accent)"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="145" y="102">Chunk: "PTO Policy v2"</text>
  </g>
  <path class="flow draw fragment" pathLength="1" d="M 500 95 L 560 95"/>
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="tag main" x="600" y="60" width="60" height="34"/>
    <text class="tagtext" x="630" y="83">[1]</text>
    <rect class="card" x="670" y="60" width="260" height="70"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="690" y="102">(PTO Policy v2) ...</text>
  </g>
  <text class="cap" x="500" y="200" text-anchor="middle">the number is the excerpt's only name inside the prompt</text>
  <text class="note" x="500" y="240" text-anchor="middle">order of chunks in decides the order of numbers out -- nothing else does</text>
</svg>
```

A chunk's doc title never reaches the model directly. Its `[n]` does.

::: notes
The numbering is positional, not semantic: chunk one becomes `[1]` because it is first in the list `assemble_prompt()` was handed, full stop. That is also why generation can later write `[2]` in its answer and the app can resolve it back to a real source: the mapping was fixed the moment the prompt was built.
:::

---

## An instruction wraps the excerpts

```{=html}
<svg class="ragfig" viewBox="0 0 1000 380">
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="zone" x="40" y="50" width="280" height="280"/>
    <text class="zonelabel" x="180" y="80" text-anchor="middle">PREAMBLE</text>
    <text class="note" x="180" y="180" text-anchor="middle">the question, plus</text>
    <text class="note" x="180" y="206" text-anchor="middle">"answer using only</text>
    <text class="note" x="180" y="232" text-anchor="middle">the excerpts below"</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="zone" x="360" y="50" width="280" height="280" style="stroke:var(--accent)"/>
    <text class="zonelabel" x="500" y="80" text-anchor="middle" style="fill:var(--accent)">EXCERPTS</text>
    <rect class="tag main" x="470" y="140" width="60" height="30"/>
    <text class="tagtext" x="500" y="161">[1]</text>
    <rect class="tag feat" x="470" y="182" width="60" height="30"/>
    <text class="tagtext" x="500" y="203">[2]</text>
    <rect class="tag head" x="470" y="224" width="60" height="30"/>
    <text class="tagtext" x="500" y="245">[3]</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="zone" x="680" y="50" width="280" height="280"/>
    <text class="zonelabel" x="820" y="80" text-anchor="middle">CLOSING RULE</text>
    <text class="note" x="820" y="180" text-anchor="middle">"cite each fact as [n].</text>
    <text class="note" x="820" y="206" text-anchor="middle">If the excerpts don't</text>
    <text class="note" x="820" y="232" text-anchor="middle">cover it, say so."</text>
  </g>
</svg>
```

::: notes
Three zones, in this exact order in the real string: an instruction that scopes the model to the excerpts, the numbered excerpts themselves, and a closing rule that demands a citation for every claim. The closing rule is what makes cite-or-refuse enforceable downstream -- it is written directly into the prompt, not bolted on after the model answers.
:::

---

## What crosses the boundary to the model

```{=html}
<svg class="ragfig" viewBox="0 0 1000 380">
  <rect class="zone" x="40" y="60" width="420" height="260"/>
  <text class="zonelabel" x="250" y="90" text-anchor="middle">EVERYTHING RAG KNOWS</text>
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="node ghost" x="80" y="120" width="150" height="55"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="100" y="153">corpus</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="node ghost" x="290" y="120" width="150" height="55"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="315" y="153">index</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="node ghost" x="80" y="200" width="360" height="55"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="100" y="233">48 chunks</text>
  </g>

  <rect class="zone" x="620" y="60" width="340" height="260" style="stroke:var(--amber)"/>
  <text class="zonelabel" x="790" y="90" text-anchor="middle" style="fill:var(--amber)">THE MODEL</text>

  <g class="fragment">
    <path class="flow draw fragment" pathLength="1" d="M 460 250 L 640 250"/>
    <rect class="card hot" x="660" y="220" width="260" height="60"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="680" y="255" style="fill:var(--amber)">the prompt string</text>
  </g>

  <text class="cap" x="500" y="355" text-anchor="middle">corpus, index, and every other chunk never cross -- only this one string does</text>
</svg>
```

The model never sees the corpus. It never sees the index. It sees one string, built once, per question.

::: notes
This is why grounding is checkable: the model's entire universe for this answer is the literal text `assemble_prompt()` returned. Nothing else it might "know" from training can slip in through this channel, because nothing else is in the channel.
:::

<!--FB:augmentation|Augmentation: Building the Prompt-->

---

# Cite or Refuse {.center}

::: lead
Retrieval and augmentation hand the model a stack of excerpts. Generation is the
last step: turn that stack into a sentence a human can trust, or say, honestly,
that the corpus doesn't have the answer.
:::

Cite or refuse is the discipline that makes a RAG system trustworthy.

::: notes
Generation is not "the model writes whatever it wants." Every claim either
traces to a retrieved chunk, or the system declines. There is no third option.
