This is the same demo query that ran through retrieval, hybrid search, and
augmentation in the earlier slides, now reaching its two possible endings.
:::

---

## Two ways a question can end

```{=html}
<svg class="ragfig" viewBox="0 0 1000 400">
  <g class="fragment">
    <text class="note" x="255" y="55" text-anchor="middle" style="fill:var(--muted)">query: "how many days of PTO do I get"</text>
    <rect class="card" x="60" y="80" width="390" height="260" style="stroke:var(--feat)"/>
    <text class="zonelabel" x="255" y="112" style="fill:var(--feat)">ANSWERED</text>
    <text class="cardtext" x="85" y="160">Full-time employees accrue 20</text>
    <text class="cardtext" x="85" y="188">days of paid time off per year.</text>
    <rect class="tag feat" x="85" y="212" width="34" height="26"/>
    <text class="tagtext" x="102" y="230">[1]</text>
    <text class="note" x="130" y="230">Paid Time Off Policy v2</text>
    <text class="note" x="255" y="316" text-anchor="middle" style="fill:var(--feat)">score clears the refusal bar</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <text class="note" x="745" y="55" text-anchor="middle" style="fill:var(--muted)">query: "what's the weather in Tokyo tomorrow"</text>
    <rect class="card" x="550" y="80" width="390" height="260" style="stroke:#e06b9c"/>
    <text class="zonelabel" x="745" y="112" style="fill:#e06b9c">REFUSED</text>
    <text class="cardtext" x="575" y="170">"The corpus doesn't cover this</text>
    <text class="cardtext" x="575" y="198">well enough to answer."</text>
    <text class="note" x="745" y="316" text-anchor="middle" style="fill:#e06b9c">score never clears the bar</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <text class="cap big" x="500" y="380">Same generator, same gate -- the evidence decides which card you land on.</text>
  </g>
</svg>
```

::: notes
Same model, same prompt template, same threshold check. The only difference
between these two cards is what retrieval found. A well-covered question gets
a cited sentence. A genuinely uncovered one gets an honest no.
:::

---

## A citation is a pointer, not decoration

```{=html}
<svg class="ragfig" viewBox="0 0 1000 400">
  <g class="fragment">
    <text class="cap big" x="500" y="90">"...accrue 20 days of paid time off per year."</text>
    <rect class="tag feat" x="710" y="66" width="46" height="30"/>
    <text class="tagtext" x="733" y="86">[1]</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <path class="leader feat" d="M 733 96 L 733 210" style="stroke:var(--feat)"/>
    <rect class="card" x="330" y="210" width="340" height="130" style="stroke:var(--feat)"/>
    <text class="zonelabel" x="500" y="242" style="fill:var(--feat)">RETRIEVED CHUNK</text>
    <text class="cardtext" x="352" y="278">Paid Time Off Policy v2</text>
    <text class="note" x="352" y="306">"...accrue 20 days of paid time off..."</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <text class="note" x="500" y="378" text-anchor="middle">[1] is a pointer, not decoration -- it names the exact chunk the sentence came from.</text>
  </g>
</svg>
```

::: notes
The bracket number is not a footnote for style. It resolves back to one
specific chunk in the index. Anyone reading the answer can walk that pointer
and check the claim against the source themselves.
:::

---

## Where the line gets drawn

```{=html}
<svg class="ragfig" viewBox="0 0 1000 400">
  <g class="fragment">
    <path class="edge" style="marker-end:none;stroke:var(--edge)" d="M 100 220 L 900 220"/>
    <text class="note" x="100" y="252">low similarity</text>
    <text class="note" x="900" y="252" text-anchor="end">high similarity</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <path class="leader head" d="M 196 190 L 196 250" style="stroke:var(--amber)"/>
    <text class="cap" x="196" y="170" style="fill:var(--amber)">refusal threshold</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <circle cx="140" cy="220" r="16" style="fill:#3a2230;stroke:#e06b9c;stroke-width:2.5"/>
    <text class="note" x="140" y="290" text-anchor="middle" style="fill:#e06b9c">weather query: 0.00</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <circle class="node feat" cx="580" cy="220" r="16"/>
    <text class="note" x="580" y="290" text-anchor="middle" style="fill:var(--feat)">PTO query: 0.48</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <text class="cap big" x="500" y="360">Below the line: refuse. Above it: cite.</text>
  </g>
</svg>
```

::: notes
The gate is one number: the best similarity score retrieval returned. Set it
too low and the model starts guessing on thin evidence. Set it too high and it
refuses questions the corpus actually answers. The threshold is a real,
tunable knob, not a metaphor.
:::

---

## No partial credit

```{=html}
<svg class="ragfig" viewBox="0 0 1000 400">
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="card" x="400" y="50" width="200" height="70"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="420" y="92">check top score</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <path class="edge" d="M 460 120 L 260 220"/>
    <rect class="card" x="80" y="220" width="320" height="110" style="stroke:var(--feat)"/>
    <text class="zonelabel" x="240" y="252" style="fill:var(--feat)">ABOVE THE BAR</text>
    <text class="cardtext" x="105" y="292">write the answer, cite [n]</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <path class="edge" d="M 540 120 L 740 220"/>
    <rect class="card" x="600" y="220" width="320" height="110" style="stroke:#e06b9c"/>
    <text class="zonelabel" x="760" y="252" style="fill:#e06b9c">BELOW THE BAR</text>
    <text class="cardtext" x="625" y="292">decline, honestly</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <text class="note" x="500" y="378" text-anchor="middle">No partial credit -- an answer is either grounded, or it doesn't happen.</text>
  </g>
</svg>
```

::: notes
A plain language model never has this branch -- it always produces text. Cite
or refuse gives the system a way to say "I don't know" instead of a fluent
guess. That one branch is the difference between a tool people can trust and
one that merely sounds confident.
:::

<!--FB:generation|Generation: Cite or Refuse-->

---

# Agentic RAG: The Tool-Use Loop {.center}

::: lead
Every concept so far runs retrieval exactly once per question. An agent gets to
check its own result first, and search again if it wasn't good enough.
:::

::: notes
This is the last mechanical concept before evaluation. Nothing about the retrieval
machinery changes here -- the same `.search()` from every earlier topic is still
doing the work. What's new is a decision wrapped around it: look at the score,
decide whether to trust it, and only pay for a second call when the first one earns it.
:::

---

## One search, or two?

The retrieval loop so far always asks once. An agent gets to check its own work first.

```{=html}
<svg class="ragfig" viewBox="0 0 1000 400">
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="card" x="60" y="90" width="380" height="220"/>
    <text class="cap big" x="250" y="140" text-anchor="middle">One-shot retrieval</text>
    <text class="note" x="250" y="185" text-anchor="middle">search once</text>
    <text class="note" x="250" y="215" text-anchor="middle">answer immediately</text>
    <text class="note" x="250" y="245" text-anchor="middle">no matter how weak the hit</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="card hot" x="560" y="90" width="380" height="220"/>
    <text class="cap big" x="750" y="140" text-anchor="middle">Agentic retrieval</text>
    <text class="note" x="750" y="185" text-anchor="middle">search, then check the score</text>
    <text class="note" x="750" y="215" text-anchor="middle">reformulate if it's weak</text>
    <text class="note" x="750" y="245" text-anchor="middle">search again before answering</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <text class="note" x="500" y="360" text-anchor="middle">The only difference: the ability to look at its own result and try again.</text>
  </g>
</svg>
```

::: notes
Topics so far (retrieval through generation) are one fixed pass: embed the query, pull the top chunks, answer. An agent wraps that same search_corpus function as a tool it can call, inspect, and call again -- the retrieval machinery underneath doesn't change, only who decides when to stop.
:::

---

## The decision loop

One tool call, one confidence check, and a fork: answer now, or search again first.

```{=html}
<svg class="ragfig" viewBox="0 0 1000 400">
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="node" x="40" y="140" width="170" height="90"/>
    <text class="cap" x="125" y="192" text-anchor="middle">question</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <path class="edge" d="M 210 185 L 278 185"/>
    <text class="note" x="245" y="165" text-anchor="middle">call</text>
    <rect class="node feat" x="280" y="140" width="230" height="90"/>
    <text class="cap" x="395" y="192" text-anchor="middle">search_corpus</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <path class="edge" d="M 510 185 L 588 185"/>
    <text class="note" x="550" y="165" text-anchor="middle">check</text>
    <polygon class="node" points="680,110 770,185 680,260 590,185"/>
    <text class="cap" x="680" y="190" text-anchor="middle">confident?</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <path class="edge" d="M 770 185 L 828 185"/>
    <text class="note" x="800" y="165" text-anchor="middle">yes</text>
    <rect class="node" x="830" y="140" width="140" height="90"/>
    <text class="cap" x="900" y="192" text-anchor="middle">answer</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <path class="flow draw fragment" pathLength="1" d="M 680 260 C 680 335, 395 335, 395 232"/>
    <text class="note" x="540" y="355" text-anchor="middle">no -> reformulate, search_corpus again</text>
  </g>
</svg>
```

::: notes
This is run_agent(): a scripted loop, not a free-ranging agent. It calls search_corpus once, and only takes the loop-back edge if the top score comes back under 0.2. The retry is capped at exactly one -- there's no path back through the diamond a second time, so this can never turn into an unbounded search spiral.
:::

---

## A real trace

The question: "what's up with the database when it just kind of goes down." Vague on purpose.

```{=html}
<svg class="ragfig" viewBox="0 0 1000 400">
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="card" x="60" y="50" width="880" height="110"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="90" y="95">search_corpus("what's up with the database ... goes down")</text>
    <text class="note" x="90" y="130">-> top: On-call Runbook: Database Outage v2, score 0.176</text>
    <text class="note" x="90" y="152" style="fill:var(--amber)">0.176 &lt; 0.2 -> not confident, reformulate</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="card hot" x="60" y="200" width="880" height="110"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="90" y="245">search_corpus("what's with database ... goes down")</text>
    <text class="note" x="90" y="280">-> top: On-call Runbook: Database Outage v2, score 0.176</text>
    <text class="note" x="90" y="302">same top doc, same score -- the reformulation confirmed it, not changed it</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <text class="cap" x="500" y="360" text-anchor="middle">finish: "If the primary database is unreachable, page the database on-call immediately. [1]"</text>
  </g>
</svg>
```

::: notes
DEMO_QUERY_WEAK ("what happens during a database outage") was the first thing tried here, and despite the name it scores 0.212 -- just over the 0.2 bar, so it never triggers a second call. This awkward, filler-heavy phrasing is what actually pushes the first score under threshold. Both calls land on the same document in this case; the reformulation didn't need to find something new, it just had to clear the confidence bar.
:::

---

## How the rewrite happens

The reformulation is a word filter, not a rewrite from scratch: drop anything four letters or shorter.

```{=html}
<svg class="ragfig" viewBox="0 0 1000 400">
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="card" x="60" y="70" width="880" height="120"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="90" y="140" xml:space="preserve"><tspan>what's </tspan><tspan style="fill:var(--muted);text-decoration:line-through">up </tspan><tspan>with </tspan><tspan style="fill:var(--muted);text-decoration:line-through">the </tspan><tspan>database when </tspan><tspan style="fill:var(--muted);text-decoration:line-through">it </tspan><tspan>just kind </tspan><tspan style="fill:var(--muted);text-decoration:line-through">of </tspan><tspan>goes down</tspan></text>
    <text class="note" x="90" y="175">grey + struck: words of length 3 or less</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <path class="edge" d="M 500 190 L 500 235"/>
    <rect class="card hot" x="60" y="245" width="880" height="90"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="90" y="298">what's with database when just kind goes down</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <text class="note" x="500" y="370" text-anchor="middle">A real agent would rewrite with an LLM call. This toy loop keeps it deterministic on purpose, so the figure is exactly reproducible.</text>
  </g>
</svg>
```

::: notes
The rule is literally `" ".join(w for w in question.split() if len(w) > 3)`. It has nothing to do with meaning -- it just strips short filler words, the same words a person trims when repeating themselves more plainly. It's a stand-in for what a real agent's reformulation step does conceptually, kept simple so the trace stays deterministic and checkable.
:::

---

## Not blind repetition

The agent only pays for a second search when the first one earns it.

```{=html}
<svg class="ragfig" viewBox="0 0 1000 400">
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="zone" x="30" y="30" width="940" height="150"/>
    <text class="zonelabel" x="500" y="55" text-anchor="middle">CONFIDENT ON THE FIRST TRY -- ONE CALL</text>
    <rect class="node" x="60" y="80" width="150" height="70"/>
    <text class="cap" x="135" y="122" text-anchor="middle">question</text>
    <path class="edge" d="M 210 115 L 268 115"/>
    <rect class="node" x="270" y="80" width="220" height="70"/>
    <text class="cap" x="380" y="122" text-anchor="middle">search_corpus</text>
    <path class="edge" d="M 490 115 L 548 115"/>
    <rect class="node" x="550" y="80" width="150" height="70"/>
    <text class="cap" x="625" y="122" text-anchor="middle">answer</text>
    <text class="note" x="850" y="122" text-anchor="middle">score 0.212</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="zone" x="30" y="210" width="940" height="170"/>
    <text class="zonelabel" x="500" y="235" text-anchor="middle">WEAK ON THE FIRST TRY -- TWO CALLS</text>
    <rect class="node" x="60" y="260" width="130" height="70"/>
    <text class="cap" x="125" y="302" text-anchor="middle">question</text>
    <path class="edge" d="M 190 295 L 238 295"/>
    <rect class="node" x="240" y="260" width="190" height="70"/>
    <text class="cap" x="335" y="302" text-anchor="middle">search_corpus</text>
    <path class="edge" d="M 430 295 L 478 295"/>
    <rect class="node feat" x="480" y="260" width="190" height="70"/>
    <text class="cap" x="575" y="302" text-anchor="middle">search again</text>
    <path class="edge" d="M 670 295 L 718 295"/>
    <rect class="node" x="720" y="260" width="130" height="70"/>
    <text class="cap" x="785" y="302" text-anchor="middle">answer</text>
    <text class="note" x="920" y="302" text-anchor="middle">0.176</text>
  </g>
</svg>
```

::: notes
Same corpus, same k, same code path -- the only thing that changes the shape of the trace is the confidence of the first hit. That's the actual point of the design: an agent that searched twice on every question would double its latency and cost for no benefit on the questions it already nailed. Gating the retry on a real threshold means the expensive path only runs when it's earning its keep.
:::

<!--FB:agentic-rag|Agentic RAG: The Tool-Use Loop-->

---

# Evaluation: How You Know It's Working {.center}

::: lead
A demo can look convincing and still be wrong. The only way to trust a RAG
system is to run it against questions with known answers, and grade it
honestly.
:::

Every concept so far -- chunking, retrieval, hybrid search, reranking,
generation -- is a design choice. Evaluation is how you find out whether
those choices actually work.

::: notes
This is the closing concept of the deck. Nothing built so far should be
trusted just because it looks reasonable in a one-off demo. A real
evaluation is a fixed, repeatable question set with a known correct answer
for each question, run automatically every time the pipeline changes.
:::

---

## A frozen set of questions, each with a known answer

Write down real questions a user would ask, then name the one document that
actually answers each. Freeze the list. Score every future change against
it, unchanged.

```{=html}
<svg class="ragfig" viewBox="0 0 1000 400">
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="card" x="40" y="30" width="560" height="60"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="66" y="67">"parental leave weeks"</text>
    <path class="edge" d="M 610 60 L 700 60"/>
    <rect class="card" x="710" y="30" width="250" height="60"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="734" y="67" style="font-size:13px">hr-06 &middot; Parental Leave Policy</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="card" x="40" y="110" width="560" height="60"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="66" y="147">"code review pull request size"</text>
    <path class="edge" d="M 610 140 L 700 140"/>
    <rect class="card" x="710" y="110" width="250" height="60"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="734" y="147" style="font-size:13px">eng-08 &middot; Code Review Guidelines</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="card" x="40" y="190" width="560" height="60"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="66" y="227">"aurora vault encryption key rotation"</text>
    <path class="edge" d="M 610 220 L 700 220"/>
    <rect class="card" x="710" y="190" width="250" height="60"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="734" y="227" style="font-size:13px">prod-08 &middot; Aurora Vault Encryption</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="card" x="40" y="290" width="560" height="60" style="stroke:var(--muted); stroke-dasharray:6 5"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="66" y="327">"what is the meaning of life"</text>
    <path class="edge" d="M 610 320 L 700 320" style="stroke-dasharray:6 5"/>
    <rect class="card" x="710" y="290" width="250" height="60" style="stroke:var(--muted); stroke-dasharray:6 5"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="734" y="315" style="font-size:12px">no such document</text>
    <text class="cardtext" x="734" y="333" style="font-size:12px">exists -- on purpose</text>
  </g>
</svg>
```

::: notes
Three of the four questions have a real answer in the corpus. The fourth is
deliberately unanswerable -- it belongs in the set for exactly the same
reason as the other three, to check what the system does, not just whether
it sounds confident.
:::

---

## Recall@k is a looser bar than faithful

Recall@k asks whether the right document is anywhere in the top few
results. Faithful asks a stricter question -- is it the very top hit, with a
real similarity score worth trusting.

```{=html}
<svg class="ragfig" viewBox="0 0 1000 400">
  <g class="fragment">
    <circle class="zone" cx="280" cy="200" r="150"/>
    <circle class="zone" cx="280" cy="200" r="100"/>
    <circle class="zone" cx="280" cy="200" r="50"/>
    <circle cx="280" cy="140" r="9" fill="var(--accent)"/>
    <text class="cap" x="280" y="370">recall@5</text>
    <text class="note" x="280" y="392" text-anchor="middle" style="font-size:13px">right doc lands anywhere in the top 5</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <circle class="zone" cx="720" cy="200" r="150"/>
    <circle class="zone" cx="720" cy="200" r="100"/>
    <circle class="zone" cx="720" cy="200" r="50"/>
    <circle cx="720" cy="200" r="9" fill="var(--feat)"/>
    <text class="cap" x="720" y="370">faithful</text>
    <text class="note" x="720" y="392" text-anchor="middle" style="font-size:13px">right doc is the #1 hit, above threshold</text>
  </g>
</svg>
```

::: notes
A pipeline can pass recall@k and still be untrustworthy at the top. The
right document is in the list, but something else outranks it -- and that
is what actually gets cited. Faithful is the harder, more honest bar.
:::

---

## Running it for real

```{=html}
<svg class="ragfig" viewBox="0 0 1000 400">
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="card" x="60" y="20" width="620" height="56"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="86" y="55">"parental leave weeks"</text>
    <circle cx="820" cy="48" r="24" fill="none" stroke="var(--feat)" stroke-width="4"/>
    <path d="M 808,48 L 817,58 L 834,36" fill="none" stroke="var(--feat)" stroke-width="4" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-linejoin="round"/>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="card" x="60" y="100" width="620" height="56"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="86" y="135">"code review pull request size"</text>
    <circle cx="820" cy="128" r="24" fill="none" stroke="var(--feat)" stroke-width="4"/>
    <path d="M 808,128 L 817,138 L 834,116" fill="none" stroke="var(--feat)" stroke-width="4" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-linejoin="round"/>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="card" x="60" y="180" width="620" height="56"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="86" y="215">"aurora vault encryption key rotation"</text>
    <circle cx="820" cy="208" r="24" fill="none" stroke="var(--feat)" stroke-width="4"/>
    <path d="M 808,208 L 817,218 L 834,196" fill="none" stroke="var(--feat)" stroke-width="4" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-linejoin="round"/>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="card" x="60" y="260" width="620" height="56" style="stroke:#e06b9c"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="86" y="295">"what is the meaning of life"</text>
    <circle cx="820" cy="288" r="24" fill="none" stroke="#e06b9c" stroke-width="4"/>
    <path d="M 808,276 L 832,300 M 832,276 L 808,300" stroke="#e06b9c" stroke-width="4" stroke-linecap="round"/>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <text class="note" x="500" y="360" text-anchor="middle">The one X is not a defect. It is the system correctly saying it does not know.</text>
  </g>
</svg>
```

::: notes
This is a real run of the real toy pipeline against these four frozen
questions. Three pass recall and faithful together. The fourth fails both,
on purpose -- there is no document in this corpus about the meaning of life,
and the honest answer is to say so, not to guess from the nearest chunk.
:::

---

## Back to where this deck started

A plain language model would have answered the meaning-of-life question
anyway, fluently and with no warning that it was guessing. This system
checks itself against real evidence first. It only speaks when it has some.

---

<!--FB:evaluation|Evaluation: How You Know It Works-->
